texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aqueous ammonia process is a promising CO2 capture technology for postcombustion flue gas treatment.  The most attractive advantage of this technology is a relative low cost of solvent regeneration compared to traditional amine solutions.  In this work, the absorption of CO2 into aqueous ammonia was experimentally studied in a gas/liquid contactor fitted with hollow fiber PTFE membranes at ambient temperature.  The absorption performance was evaluated in terms of KGav . Experimental results indicated aqueous ammonia can absorb CO2 in a hollow fiber membrane contactor over a wide range of experimental conditions.  The value of KG ranged from 1.06×10 -4 to 2.89×10 -4 m/s.  The impacts of operating parameters including CO2 partial pressure, liquid flow rate, ammonia concentration and inlet CO2 solution loading were evaluated.  The parametric impacts are similar to those of traditional amine-based absorption processes.  Although the reactivity of aqueous ammonia is moderately lower than MEA, the KGav value of aqueous ammonia is the same order of magnitude as that of MEA under the same operating conditions.  As the liquid flow rate increases aqueous ammonia can achieve a performance comparable to MEA solvent.  Long-term stability tests showed the absorption performance of aqueous ammonia remained constant in the first hour and then gradually decreased over time.  Precipitation of ammonium salts was observed on the membrane surface of the shell side, which caused membrane fouling and may facilitate membrane wetting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the increasing worldwide energy demand, more and more carbon dioxide (CO2) has been emitted into the air from existing stationary fuel-combustion sources. According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), annual CO2 emissions from fuel combustion sources have increased up to 32 Gt in 2013, accounting for 46% of global CO2 emissions [1].  Considering the huge emission amount, CO2 is deemed as a major greenhouse gas contributor to climate change.  Many governments have developed or are developing legislation for controlling CO2 emissions from industrial sources, especially coal-fired power plants.  In order to meet the stringent regulation requirements, economic and efficient carbon capture technologies are needed.

texts.orig: Chemical absorption is the most mature technology for CO2 capture since it has been successfully applied for acid gas removal in natural gas industry for many years.  The reactive nature of this technology is typically suited for capturing CO2 from power plant flue gas, which is a dilute and low-pressure gas stream.  However, the disadvantage of this technology is considerable associated capital and operating costs, mainly from the energy consumption of amine solvent regeneration.  For example, the application of this technology may lead to net efficiency penalties between 8.7% and 12% for coal-fired power generation [2].  Currently, a lot of research effort has focused on developing new solvents that offer high absorption rates, require lower energy levels for regeneration, and are resistant to oxygen and thermal degradation [38].  In recent years, the aqueous ammonia process has been widely studied due to its significant low energy consumption for the regeneration of aqueous ammonia solvent [9-14].  Differing from the chilled ammonia process, the aqueous ammonia process is operated at ambient temperature to increase the reactivity and eliminate ammonium solids precipitation.  Research results have showed many advantages of aqueous ammonia process compared to traditional amine-based absorption process.  The main advantages include:
texts.text: Chemical absorption is the most mature technology for CO2 capture since it has been successfully applied for acid gas removal in natural gas industry for many years.  The reactive nature of this technology is typically suited for capturing CO2 from power plant flue gas, which is a dilute and low-pressure gas stream.  However, the disadvantage of this technology is considerable associated capital and operating costs, mainly from the energy consumption of amine solvent regeneration.  For example, the application of this technology may lead to net efficiency penalties between 8.7% and 12% for coal-fired power generation [2].  Currently, a lot of research effort has focused on developing new solvents that offer high absorption rates, require lower energy levels for regeneration, and are resistant to oxygen and thermal degradation [38].  In recent years, the aqueous ammonia process has been widely studied due to its significant low energy consumption for the regeneration of aqueous ammonia solvent [9-14].  Differing from the chilled ammonia process, the aqueous ammonia process is operated at ambient temperature to increase the reactivity and eliminate ammonium solids precipitation.  Research results have showed many advantages of aqueous ammonia process compared to traditional amine-based absorption process.  The main advantages include:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1) A high CO2 absorption capacity, up to 1.20 kg CO2/kg ammonia, which is 3 times the monoethanolamine (MEA) capacity [15];
texts.text: A high CO2 absorption capacity, up to 1.20 kg CO2/kg ammonia, which is 3 times the monoethanolamine (MEA) capacity [15];

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2) Comparable CO2 absorption performance, the absorption rate of CO2 by 10 wt% ammonia solutions is the same order of magnitude to the one by 30 wt% MEA solutions at 314 K in a wetted wall reactor [16];
texts.text: Comparable CO2 absorption performance, the absorption rate of CO2 by 10 wt% ammonia solutions is the same order of magnitude to the one by 30 wt% MEA solutions at 314 K in a wetted wall reactor [16];

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3) Less substantial energy requirement for solvent regeneration [17, 18]. Moreover, the absorption products (ammonium carbonate and bicarbonate) can be used as fertilizers in some developing countries, which will significantly reduce the CO2 capture cost since the solvent regeneration process is not necessary [19].

Moreover, the absorption products (ammonium carbonate and bicarbonate) can be used as fertilizers in some developing countries, which will significantly reduce the CO2 capture cost since the solvent regeneration process is not necessary [19].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4) Much lower solvent cost compared to other existing and new solvents [18];
texts.text: Much lower solvent cost compared to other existing and new solvents [18];

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5) No operating problems, such as oxygen and thermal degradation and corrosion problems [20];
texts.text: No operating problems, such as oxygen and thermal degradation and corrosion problems [20];

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6) Capable to capture multi-pollutants, such as sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides. This can reduce the CO2 capture system capital cost since an auxiliary sulfur scrubbing system would not be required [18].

texts.orig: The major drawback of the aqueous ammonia process is high NH3 losses due to its low vapor pressure [21].  The NH3 that slips to the gas phase can react with CO2 to produce ammonium salts, which may cause some operating problems such as pipe blocking.  Water washing can be an effective measure to reduce NH3 loss from the absorption liquid.  Due to the advantages mentioned above, the aqueous ammonia process has been studied for CO2 capture in different gas/liquid contactors through experiments or mathematical simulations, including packed columns, spray columns and wetted wall columns [16, 22, 23].

texts.orig: Little research work has studied CO2 absorption with aqueous ammonia in a hollow fiber membrane contactor . Hollow fiber membrane contactors can offer a higher mass transfer contacting area and achieve a faster mass transfer rate compared to traditional packed columns [24, 25].  Additionally, the membrane in the membrane module separates the gas and liquid phases, limiting the NH3 slip from liquid phase to gas phase.  Development of an ammonia-based membrane absorption process may provide a higher CO2 absorption performance with less capital and operating costs than the traditional CO2 absorption process. Makhloufi et al. [26] was the first to study CO2 absorption in aqueous ammonia with a hollow fiber membrane contactor. Their results showed the polypropylene (PP) membranes did not generate stable CO2 absorption performance due to membrane fouling caused by ammonium salt precipitation.  Membrane properties, such as porosity, inner diameter of the hollow membrane fiber, and membrane pore size, have a significant impact on the CO2 absorption performance and long-term stability [27].  In general, PP membrane have a much smaller inner fiber diameter than polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membranes and they are prone to membrane fouling if precipitation of ammonia salts occurs. PTFE membranes with a large inner fiber diameter may be a suitable membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: material for CO2 absorption into aqueous ammonia and avoiding membrane fouling. Therefore, the objective of this research is to study the CO2 absorption performance with aqueous ammonia in a hollow fiber membrane contactor fitted with PTFE membranes.  The research work consisted of four tasks.  Task 1 examined the feasibility of CO2 absorption with aqueous ammonia in a used PTFE hollow fiber membrane contactor.  This work established a solid foundation for further more detailed experimental studies.  Task 2 completed a full set of CO2 absorption experiments in a membrane contactor using aqueous ammonia solutions with new PTFE membranes. Parametric impacts, including inlet CO2 partial pressure, liquid velocity, solvent concentration, and inlet CO2 solution loading, were evaluated at ambient temperature. Additionally, membrane resistance was estimated by using the Wilson plot.  Task 3 conducted CO2 absorption experiments with MEA in the same PTFE membrane contactor under the same experimental conditions of Task 2.  The MEA solvent functioned as a benchmark to compare with and assess the absorption performance of aqueous ammonia solvent.  In Task 4 a long-term performance study was conducted to assess the stability of CO2 absorption with aqueous ammonia in the PTFE membrane contactor.

texts.orig: The overall reaction rate of CO2 absorption in the ammonia solution is determined by two reactions: bicarbonate formation between CO2 and hydroxide ion, and the reaction between CO2 and ammonia [14].  The reaction between CO2 and ammonia is similar to the one between CO2 and MEA, which is via the zwitterion mechanism.  At first, CO2 reacts with ammonia to form a zwitterion.  Next, the zwitterion is deprotonated by bases in the solution.  In the aqueous ammonia solution, water and ammonia were considered as dominant bases, which contribute to the zwitterion deprotonation [16, 28]. texts.orig: This study adopted the gas absorption membrane module originally designed by deMontigny [29].  Fig. 2 shows the specification of a single membrane module. This membrane module was made from acrylic since it is chemically compatible with alkanolamine solutions and allows visible observation of internal shell side activity as well as inspection of the membrane conditions.  A membrane cartridge was inserted into the module and sealed at both ends with 28 mm rubber O-rings.  A new membrane cartridge was made through potting each end of membranes to an acrylic disc.  In order to ensure no leakage between the lumen side and shell side, the membranes were potted by applying an epoxy resin (Loctite E-60NC Hysol ® epoxy). In this study PTFE membranes were selected since they offer the largest inner fiber diameter and most durable performance compared to other general membrane materials.  The characteristics of the PTFE membranes are shown in Table 1.  A detailed procedure of membrane potting can be referred from [29].

texts.orig: A number of chemicals were purchased and used for the CO2 absorption experiment. The mixed gas stream included air and CO2.  The absorption solvents were aqueous MEA and aqueous ammonia solutions.  Hydrochloric acid (0.1 kmol/m 3 ) and methyl orange were used for liquid sample titration.  Sulfuric acid solution (40%) was used to analyze inlet CO2 loading and remove gaseous NH3 from the treated gas stream. Aqueous solutions of these chemicals were prepared to desired concentrations by diluting with de-ionized water provided in the laboratory.  The inlet CO2 loadings were prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of ammonium bicarbonate solids into aqueous ammonia solutions.  Properties of these chemicals are presented in Table 2.

texts.orig: A previously used PTFE membrane cartridge was used to test the compatibility between the membrane cartridge and the aqueous ammonia solution.  As shown in Fig. 4, the membrane cartridge consists of PTFE membranes and two acrylic discs.  Both ends of membrane fibers were potted through the holes of the acrylic discs via a Loctite E-60NC Hysol ® epoxy.  One end of this membrane cartridge was immersed in a highly concentrated aqueous ammonia solution (8.5 kmol/m 3 ) in a 250 cm 3 beaker at 295.15 K.  Observation of the membrane cartridge shows no visual changes even though the membrane cartridge had been in contact with aqueous ammonia for 48 hours.  This visual inspection indicated that the membrane cartridge including the acrylic disc, Loctite E-60NC Hysol ® epoxy, and PTFE membranes was resistant to the aqueous ammonia.  Thus, this membrane cartridge was used for subsequent ammoniabased absorption experiments.

texts.orig: Makhloufi et al. [26] studied the feasibility of CO2 absorption into the aqueous ammonia in a PP membrane contactor.  Their experimental results showed the PP membranes could not maintain a stable CO2 absorption performance since the membrane pores were easily blocked resulting from the ammonium salt precipitation. In this study, the feasibility of CO2 absorption into aqueous ammonia solutions was first evaluated by using a previously used PTFE membrane cartridge.  The experimental results indicate that the aqueous ammonia solution can offer a stable performance for CO2 absorption in the PTFE membrane contactor.  As shown in Fig. 5, the KGav value ranges from 0.26 to 0.43 kmol/m 3 -hr-kPa as the liquid flow rate is increased from 1.05×10 -6 to 3.15×10 -6 m 3 /s.  However, during the experiments, some aqueous ammonia solution was found gradually permeating through the membrane pores to the shell side at the top of some membrane fibers.  This phenomenon indicated that some membrane fibers were completely wetted.  This is most likely due to the significant hydrophobicity reduction of the used membranes considering they have been used with various ammine solvents in a large number of experiments in the past. Since wetted membranes would result in a significant reduction of CO2 absorption performance, this used PTFE membrane cartridge was not used for evaluating the CO2 absorption performance of the aqueous ammonia solution [33, 34]. A new membrane cartridge was made and used for all subsequent experiments.

texts.orig: The effect of inlet CO2 partial pressure was examined by conducting CO2 absorption experiments at three inlet CO2 partial pressures including 10 kPa, 15 kPa, and 20 kPa. As seen in Fig. 6, the inlet CO2 partial pressure has a great impact on the overall mass transfer performance.  At a given liquid flow rate the KGav value decreased as the inlet CO2 partial pressure increased from 10 kPa to 20 kPa.  Fig. 7 shows a clearer trend by plotting the KGav against the inlet CO2 partial pressure.  This trend holds true at all liquid flow rates.  The driving force for CO2 absorption process is the CO2 concentration gradient between the gas and liquid phases.  Increasing the driving force by raising the inlet CO2 partial pressure will allow more CO2 to transfer from the gas bulk to the gas/liquid interface, leading to an increase in the overall masstransfer rate of CO2.  However, the experimental results indicate the increase of the overall mass transfer rate is restricted by the limited diffusion rate of solvent molecules and chemical reaction rate in the liquid phase. This is evidenced by the reported diffusion coefficients of ammonia and CO2 in the water, i.e., 1.64×10 -9 m 2 /s and 1.92×10 -9 m 2 /s, respectively [35].  In addition, Derks and Versteeg reported the reaction rate of ammonia with CO2 is only 33% of MEA at 25 ºC [14]. texts.orig: The effect of liquid flow rate on the KGav is shown in Fig. 6 at different inlet CO2 partial pressures.  It is clear that an increase in the liquid flow rate leads to an increase in KGav values.  This trend holds true for all other experimental conditions.  Since the reaction between CO2 and aqueous ammonia is fast [14], the overall mass transfer rate of CO2 depends on the amount of CO2 transferred from the gas bulk to the gas/liquid interface and the available reactive ammonia molecules at the interface.  When increasing the liquid flow rate, the boundary layer thickness of the liquid phase is decreased.  The liquid-phase mass transfer resistance is decreased.  As a result, more ammonia molecules are diffused to and react with CO2 at the gas/liquid contacting interface.  Therefore, the KGav value increases as the liquid flow rate increases.  This phenomenon holds true in most acid/base gas absorption processes.

texts.orig: The major disadvantage of aqueous ammonia solvent for CO2 absorption is the NH3 vaporization due to its high vapor pressure at a room temperature.  Using a high concentration of aqueous ammonia solution in the CO2 absorption process will lead to a large amount of gaseous NH3 slip into the treated gas stream, which means a higher rate of solvent loss during the absorption process.  In addition, research results revealed that when the ammonia concentration is higher than 4.0 kmol/m 3 , an increase in ammonia concentration has just a slight effect on the CO2 removal efficiency but it may aggravate the NH3 loss [35].  Thus this study used a dilute ammonia solution in order to mitigate the NH3 loss as much as possible during the membrane absorption process.  As shown in Fig. 8, the KGav value increases as the ammonia concentration increases from 1.0 to 3.0 kmol/m 3 at a given liquid flow rate. The relationship between the KGav and the ammonia concentration was shown in Fig. 9.  Similar to the traditional amine solvents, an increase of ammonia concentration yields a greater amount of reactive ammonia molecules to diffuse to the gas/liquid interface and react with CO2.  As a result, the enhancement factor is increased.  The KGav values increases as the enhancement factor is increased.

texts.orig: It is also important to note that the inlet CO2 loading at different liquid flow rates has a different degree of effect on the KGav value at the same concentration of ammonia. According to Fig. 11, the KGav value is less affected by the inlet CO2 loading at a low liquid flow rate compared to that at a high liquid flow rate.  This phenomenon can be explained by considering the relationship of two factors including the liquid phase mass transfer resistance and available reactive ammonia molecules at the gas/liquid interface.  As discussed earlier, the liquid phase mass transfer resistance at a high liquid flow rate is lower than that at a low liquid flow rate.  Thus changing the inlet CO2 loading at a low liquid flow rate will generate a smaller amount of reactive ammonia molecules to diffuse to the gas/liquid contacting interface due to the high mass transfer resistance compared to that at a high liquid flow rate with a low mass transfer resistance.  Therefore, the effect of inlet CO2 loading on the KGav value at a high liquid flow rate is much more significant than that at a low liquid flow rate due to the difference of the liquid-phase mass transfer resistance.

texts.orig: 4.3 Performance comparison of aqueous ammonia and MEA
texts.text: 4.3 Performance comparison of aqueous ammonia and MEA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since traditional amine solvents have been successfully applied for acid gas absorption in many industries, performance evaluation of new solvents is generally achieved by comparing with amine solvents.  Among these amine solvents, MEA is the most widely used primary amine with a superior absorption rate.  It is often considered as the benchmark for comparing with other solvents.  The CO2 absorption rate of aqueous ammonia has previously been compared with different amines such as MEA, MDEA, and AMP in various types of gas/liquid contactors [17, 36, 37]. However, little research has been conducted for a gas/liquid membrane contactor. Mehdipour et al. [38] compared the absorption performance of aqueous ammonia and DEA through a mathematical model.  However, no valid experimental data supported their conclusion.  In this study, CO2 absorption experiments were continued to use MEA as a solvent under the same experimental conditions as the ammonia-based absorption experiments.  The absorption performance of aqueous ammonia was experimentally compared with MEA solvent.

However, under the non-wetted mode the membrane resistance only accounts for 1.8% of the overall mass transfer resistance.  This indicates that under the non-wetted mode operation the additional membrane resistance is small and negligible.  In order to avoid the membrane wetting issue, the pressure applied on the liquid phase should be controlled lower than the breakthrough pressure.

texts.orig: As seen in Fig. 17, visual observation confirmed that this decreased performance over time was caused by the formation of ammonium salt precipitated inside of the membrane pores and on the membrane surface of the module shell side.  The precipitation of ammonium salts is due to high NH3 vaporization from the liquid phase to the gas phase and caused a serious membrane fouling problem.  The precipitation of ammonium salts on the membranes may reduce the hydrophobicity of the membrane surface and further facilitate membrane wetting.  As a result, the membrane's performance degraded gradually along with membrane fouling and membrane wetting.  A similar observation of ammonium salt precipitation has been reported by Makhloufi et al. [26]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: PTFE membranes under different experimental conditions.  At ambient temperature the KGav value ranges from 0.09 to 0.25 kmol/m 3 -hr-kPa.  The effects of operating parameters on the KGav , including inlet CO2 partial pressure, aqueous ammonia concentration, liquid flow rate, and inlet CO2 solution loading, have similar trends to those of amine-based absorption processes.  By comparing the absorption performance of aqueous ammonia and MEA under the same experimental conditions, it was found that the KGav value of aqueous ammonia is in the same order of magnitude as MEA.  At the maximum liquid flow rate, aqueous ammonia can achieve 98% of absorption performance of MEA solvent.  The analysis of membrane resistance indicated that the membrane pores were partially wetted during all experiments.  The long-term stability study showed the membrane absorption performance remained constant within the first hour of operation and then gradually decreased over time due to the precipitation of ammonium salts.  The precipitation of ammonium salts caused a visible membrane fouling problem and may reduce the hydrophobicity of the membrane surface and further facilitate membrane wetting.  In summary, the ammonia-based membrane absorption process is a feasible and promising technology for post-combustion CO2 capture considering its comparable absorption performance to MEA but relatively low cost of solvent regeneration.
